content:
Authentication feels complicated until you stop thinking about libraries and start thinking about trust.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

How Authentication Works in Next.jsThe first time I tried implementing authentication in a Next.js application, I made the same mistake many developers make.
I focused on the code before understanding the flow.
I spent hours reading documentation about sessions, tokens, cookies, middleware, providers, and callbacks. Everything seemed connected, yet somehow nothing made sense.
Then I stopped looking at authentication as a collection of APIs and started looking at it as a trust system.
Suddenly, everything clicked.
If you’re new to authentication in Next.js, this mental model will help you understand what’s happening behind the scenes before diving into any specific library.
What Authentication Actually Means
Authentication answers one simple question:
“Who is this user?”
When someone logs into your application, the server needs a reliable way to verify their identity.
